title: Vaccinium micranthum
intro: Vaccinium micranthum, also known as the small-flowered blueberry, is a species of flowering plant in the heath family, Ericaceae. The leaves and fruits of V. micranthum have been traditionally used to treat various health conditions, including inflammation and gastrointestinal issues, due to its potential antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. The bioactive constituents of V. micranthum include anthocyanins, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. Vaccinium micranthum is often used in herbal preparations, such as teas, infusions, and extracts, and is also used in traditional folk medicine to treat conditions like diarrhea and dyspepsia.
taxonomy: Vaccinium micranthum is classified as a species of shrub in the family Ericaceae. This plant is native to the Andes mountain range in South America and is a member of the Vaccinium genus, which includes several species of blueberries and cranberries. The exact classification of Vaccinium micranthum can be further broken down into its binomial nomenclature, which is a unique combination of genus and species names. At the rank of genus, it belongs to the tribe Vaccinieae and is placed within the subfamily Vaccinioideae. Overall, Vaccinium micranthum is a well-defined species within the Ericaceae family.
